title: How the body develops chronic headaches from jaw clenching
author: Kalesh

 You wake up and your temples throb. Or maybe it's a dull ache behind your eyes that never...
htmlContent:
Your jaw is holding a secret. And it's giving you a headache.

You wake up and your temples throb. Or maybe it's a dull ache behind your eyes that never really leaves. You've tried caffeine, you've tried ibuprofen, you've tried sleeping more. Nothing sticks. Here's the thing. Your jaw might be the culprit. And your jaw is clenching because your nervous system is stuck in a loop it can't find its way out of.

I know this because I've lived it. For years I woke up with my teeth pressed together so hard my molars ached. My dentist pointed to the wear patterns on my enamel and asked if I was stressed. I laughed. Stressed? I was a walking pressure cooker. But I didn't connect the grinding to the headaches that followed me through every afternoon like a shadow I couldn't shake.

Let's look at what's actually happening when your jaw clenches. Because it's not just about stress. It's about how your body learned to survive.

The nervous system connection you can't ignore

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to threat. When you're in danger, your body prepares to fight, flee, or freeze. Your jaw clenches as part of that preparation. It's a primitive reflex. Think about it. If you were about to be attacked, your jaw would tighten to protect your airway, your teeth would brace for impact. That's the body doing its job.

The problem is when the threat never goes away. When you live in a state of chronic vigilance, your nervous system stays locked in that protective mode. Your jaw stays tight. And your head pays the price.

I've had days where I catch myself clenching while I'm just sitting at my desk, reading an email, making dinner. My body doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. It just knows it needs to stay ready. So it tightens everything. My shoulders, my neck, my jaw. And the tension radiates upward into my skull, creating what feels like a band of pressure around my head.

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma and chronic stress get stored in the body. Your jaw is one of the primary storage sites. When you don't complete the stress response cycle, when you can't fight or flee, the energy stays locked in your tissues. Your jaw muscles stay contracted. And over time, that constant contraction leads to inflammation, nerve irritation, and yes, chronic headaches.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium helps relax muscle tissue and supports the nervous system's ability to shift out of that hypervigilant state. It's not a cure, but it can take the edge off. I take it before bed and notice my jaw is less tight in the morning.

How clenching becomes a headache machine

Here's the mechanics of it. Your temporomandibular joint, the hinge connecting your jaw to your skull, is surrounded by muscles that control chewing, talking, and facial expression. When you clench, those muscles contract and stay contracted. That constant tension pulls on the bones and tissues around your temples, your forehead, the back of your neck. Over time, the muscles develop trigger points, small knots of hyperirritable tissue that refer pain to other areas of your head.

You might feel the pain in your temples. Or behind your eyes. Or at the base of your skull where your neck meets your head. Sometimes it feels like a tension headache, sometimes like a migraine. The location shifts because the tension radiates along fascial lines, the connective tissue web that runs through your entire body.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), writes extensively about how trauma s physically. He notes that chronic muscle tension is one of the most common somatic symptoms of unresolved stress. Your jaw clenching isn't a bad habit. It's a survival strategy that outlived its usefulness. Your body is still bracing for a threat that may have passed years ago.

I remember sitting in my car after a therapy session, realizing my jaw was clenched so hard my teeth ached. I hadn't noticed until I consciously tried to relax it. That's the insidious part. The clenching becomes so automatic, so woven into your daily experience, that you stop feeling it as a separate sensation. It just becomes your normal. And your headaches become your normal too.

But here's what I want you to hear. Normal doesn't mean healthy. And it doesn't mean permanent.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

What actually helps when your jaw won't let go

I'm not going to tell you to just relax your jaw. That's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. Your jaw is clenched because your nervous system believes it needs to be. You can't override that with conscious effort alone. You need to address the system underneath.

Start with awareness. Not judgment, just noticing. Several times a day, pause and check in with your jaw. Is it tight? Are your teeth touching? Most of the time, they shouldn't be. When your mouth is at rest, your teeth should be slightly apart, your lips closed, your tongue resting gently on the roof of your mouth. That's the resting position. If your teeth are touching, you're clenching, even if it's light.

Once you notice, you can experiment with gentle movement. Open your mouth slowly, then close it. Move your jaw side to side. Yawn deliberately. These small movements signal to your nervous system that it's safe to release. They break the holding pattern.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). Extended exhales activate the vagus nerve and shift you toward the parasympathetic state, the rest and digest mode where your body can stop bracing. I use mine when I notice my jaw tightening during the day. A few slow breaths and I can feel the tension in my face soften.

Another practical step is reducing sensory input that keeps your nervous system on high alert. Loud noises, bright lights, constant notifications, all of these can maintain that hypervigilant state. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient noise without blocking everything out. I wear them at coffee shops, on public transit, even at home when the world feels too loud. It gives my nervous system a break.

The bigger picture your jaw is pointing to

Here's the uncomfortable truth. Your chronic headaches from jaw clenching are not a problem to be fixed. They're a message. Your body is telling you that something in your life, your environment, your relationships, your internal world, is keeping you in a state of threat. The clenching is the symptom. The headache is the alarm.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

I know you don't want to hear that. I didn't either. I wanted a quick fix, a mouthguard, a pill, a technique that would make it stop without having to look at the deeper stuff. But here's what I learned. The deeper stuff doesn't go away just because you ignore it. It just finds another way to speak.

Maybe your jaw clenching is about perfectionism, the constant pressure to get everything right. Maybe it's about people-pleasing, the way you hold back what you really want to say. Maybe it's about grief you haven't let yourself feel, anger you've swallowed for years. Your jaw is the gatekeeper. And when the gate stays locked, the pressure builds.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who works with structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of us hold different survival responses. One part might be the clencher, the one that keeps you safe by keeping you tight. Another part might be the headache sufferer, the one that carries the pain. These parts aren't enemies. They're trying to help. But they're stuck in an old strategy.

Sit with that for a moment. What would it mean to listen to your jaw instead of fighting it? What would it mean to ask your body what it's trying to protect you from?

I'm not saying the headaches will disappear overnight. They probably won't. But when you start treating the clenching as a signal rather than a malfunction, something shifts. You stop being at war with your own body. You start being curious. And curiosity, believe it or not, is a form of safety.

Your nervous system can feel the difference between someone trying to force it to relax and someone who is simply paying attention, without agenda, without judgment. That attention is the first step toward real change.
